About this marking scheme
The purpose of this marking scheme is to provide teachers, learners and other interested parties
with an understanding of the assessment criteria used to assess this specific assessment.
A list of indicative content suggests the range of economic concepts, theory, issues and arguments
which might be included in learners' answers. It is not intended to be exhaustive and learners do not
have to include all of the indicative content to reach the highest level of the mark scheme.
The level-based mark schemes sub-divide the total mark to allocate to individual assessment
objectives. A mark will be awarded for each assessment objective targeted in the question, then
totalled to give an overall mark for the question.


                              © Predicted Paper – in the style of WJEC/Eduqas

GENERAL MARKING GUIDANCE
Positive Marking
It should be remembered that learners are writing under examination conditions and credit should
be given for what the learner writes, rather than adopting the approach of penalising for any
omissions.

SECTION B
21. Using the data, explain the likely link between the two charts. [4]


 Ba                AO1                               AO2                               AO3
 nd

  2                                                                       2 marks
                                                                          Good analysis
                                                                          A clear chain of reasoning
                                                                          links higher Fed rates to dollar
                                                                          appreciation via capital flows.

  1    1 mark                          1 mark                             1 mark
       Limited knowledge               Limited application                Limited analysis
       Understanding of the link       The data is used in the            A chain of reasoning exists
       between interest rates and      answer.                            but is underdeveloped.
       exchange rates.

  0    0 marks                         0 marks                            0 marks


Indicative content
AO1
Interest rate parity theory suggests that capital will flow towards currencies offering higher risk-
adjusted returns. Higher US interest rates relative to the Eurozone raise the returns on dollar-
denominated assets, increasing demand for dollars (and supply of euros) on the foreign exchange
market.
AO2
The data shows the Fed funds rate rising from 0.25% to 5.5% between early 2022 and mid-2024.
Over the same period, the USD appreciated against the EUR (the EUR/USD rate fell from 1.13 to
around 1.05 before partially recovering and then falling again to 1.07 in mid-2024).
AO3
Chain: aggressive Fed tightening raises the interest rate differential between the US and the
Eurozone → hot money / portfolio capital flows into dollar assets → demand curve for dollars shifts
right / supply of dollars shifts left → dollar appreciates against the euro. The partial recovery of the
euro in 2023 is consistent with the ECB catching up in its own tightening cycle, narrowing the
differential.
Allow other relevant points.

22. Using the diagram, discuss whether a carbon tax is the best policy response to industrial
pollution. [7]


 Ba                 AO2                                   AO3                                AO4
 nd

  3                                                                             3 marks
                                                                                Excellent evaluation
                                                                                Clear supported judgement
                                                                                about whether a carbon tax is
                                                                                the best policy, considering
                                                                                information failures and
                                                                                alternatives.

  2    2 marks                              2 marks                             2 marks
       Good application                     Good analysis                       Good evaluation
       The diagram is used                  Well-developed chain of             Clear counterarguments
       effectively to support the           reasoning about how a carbon        showing the limitations of a
       argument.                            tax internalises the externality.   carbon tax and alternative
                                                                                policies.

  1    1 mark                               1 mark                              1 mark
       Limited application                  Limited analysis                    Limited evaluation
       The diagram is referenced            The chain of reasoning is           Counterarguments present
       superficially.                       underdeveloped.                     but not developed.

  0    0 marks                              0 marks                             0 marks


Indicative content
AO2
The diagram shows a market with MSC > MPC, reflecting the negative production externality
(emissions, air quality damage, climate costs). Free market equilibrium Qm exceeds the social
optimum Qs. A welfare loss triangle lies between MSC and MSB from Qs to Qm. A carbon tax equal
to the marginal external cost at Qs would shift the MPC curve up to MPC + t = MSC, internalising
the externality and restoring the socially optimum output.
AO3
A Pigouvian (carbon) tax aligns private incentives with social costs: firms face the full cost of their
emissions, so profit-maximising decisions produce at the social optimum. The tax raises revenue
that can fund green investment or offset distortionary taxes (double dividend). It preserves market
incentives for cost-minimising abatement – firms choose the cheapest way to reduce emissions, so
the policy is productively efficient relative to command-and-control regulation.
AO4
Counterarguments: (i) Setting the 'correct' tax rate is extremely difficult due to uncertainty over the
marginal damage – information failure. (ii) PED for polluting goods may be very inelastic in the short
run so a tax has little effect on quantity, only raising prices. (iii) Regressive impact – lower-income
households spend a higher share of income on energy, creating equity concerns unless revenue is
recycled. (iv) Carbon leakage – domestic firms may relocate production to jurisdictions without a
carbon tax, reducing competitiveness and failing to cut global emissions. Carbon border adjustment
mechanisms can mitigate this but are administratively complex. (v) Alternatives: tradable emissions
permits (cap-and-trade, e.g. EU ETS) fix the quantity of emissions at the socially optimal level,
letting price adjust – arguably better when the total emissions target is known. Direct regulation
(emissions standards), subsidies for green alternatives, information campaigns, and investment in
R&D and infrastructure are also options. (vi) Political economy – tax changes are politically

                                    © Predicted Paper – in the style of WJEC/Eduqas
                                                             MARKING SCHEME – A520U10-1 (Predicted – Hard)

contentious and may be reversed. The 'best' policy likely involves a mix: a carbon price as the
backbone, complemented by R&D support and regulations for harder-to-abate sectors.
Allow other relevant points.


                               © Predicted Paper – in the style of WJEC/Eduqas
                                                              MARKING SCHEME – A520U10-1 (Predicted – Hard)

23. Use the data to explain where the Nash equilibrium lies in this matrix and why collusion
between the firms might be difficult to sustain. [4]


 Ba            AO1 (1 mark)                     AO2 (2 marks)                      AO3 (1 mark)
 nd

  2                                     2 marks
                                        Good application
                                        The matrix is used to show
                                        the Nash equilibrium and why
                                        collusion is unstable (cheating
                                        incentive).

  1    1 mark                           1 mark                             1 mark
       Limited knowledge                Limited application                Limited analysis
       Understanding of Nash            The matrix data is used to         A clear chain of reasoning
       equilibrium and/or collusion.    identify the equilibrium.          about how the equilibrium is
                                                                           reached or why collusion fails.

  0    0 marks                          0 marks                            0 marks


Indicative content
AO1
A Nash equilibrium is a combination of strategies where no player can gain by unilaterally deviating.
Tacit or explicit collusion would allow both firms to charge a high price, but this is unstable because
each firm has a unilateral incentive to cheat.
AO2 / AO3
Testing each cell: if SouthFly sets High, NorthAir compares £80m (High) and £110m (Low) –
chooses Low. If SouthFly sets Low, NorthAir compares £30m (High) and £50m (Low) – chooses
Low. Low is NorthAir's dominant strategy. By symmetry, Low is also SouthFly's dominant strategy.
The Nash equilibrium is (Low, Low) → £50m each.
The collusive outcome (High, High) would give £80m each – mutually better than £50m. But it is not
a Nash equilibrium because each firm can unilaterally defect from High to Low and earn £110m
instead of £80m. This is the classic prisoners' dilemma. Collusion is difficult to sustain because (i)
the gain from cheating in a single round is large; (ii) detecting cheating quickly is difficult; (iii) legal
prohibitions on price-fixing make communication illegal; (iv) entry of new firms or demand shocks
destabilise any agreement. Only with repeated interactions and credible punishment strategies (e.g.
tit-for-tat) might cooperation be sustained.
Allow other relevant points.


                                © Predicted Paper – in the style of WJEC/Eduqas
                                                              MARKING SCHEME – A520U10-1 (Predicted – Hard)

24. Using the data, discuss whether the Phillips curve remains a useful framework for
understanding the inflation-unemployment relationship in the UK. [8]


 Ba                AO2                                AO3                               AO4
 nd

  3                                     3 marks                            3 marks
                                        Excellent analysis                 Excellent evaluation
                                        Developed chains covering          Supported judgement about
                                        short-run and long-run Phillips    the usefulness of the
                                        curves, role of expectations       framework and its limitations.
                                        and supply shocks.

  2    2 marks                          2 marks                            2 marks
       Good application                 Good analysis                      Good evaluation
       Data is used effectively to      Developed chain of reasoning       Clear counterarguments
       support analysis/evaluation.     about the Phillips curve           challenging the simple Phillips
                                        relationship.                      curve relationship.

  1    1 mark                           1 mark                             1 mark
       Limited application.             Limited analysis.                  Limited evaluation.

  0    0 marks                          0 marks                            0 marks


Indicative content
AO2
The data shows an atypical pattern: inflation spiking to 9.1% in 2022 while unemployment fell to
3.7% (simple Phillips relationship holds), but unemployment remained low (4.0%) even as inflation
fell sharply back to 7.3% in 2023 and 2.5% in 2024. The extract highlights exogenous supply shocks
(energy prices, supply bottlenecks) as the main driver of 2022 inflation.
AO3
The original Phillips curve (1958) identified an empirical inverse relationship between wage/price
inflation and unemployment – low unemployment implies tight labour markets and rising wages,
passed through to prices. This is the 'short-run Phillips curve' (SRPC). In 2021–22, the UK fits this
pattern to some extent – tight labour markets, high vacancy-to-unemployment ratio contributed to
wage growth. The expectations-augmented Phillips curve (Friedman/Phelps) recognises that
workers and firms adjust expectations, so the SRPC shifts over time. The long-run Phillips curve
(LRPC) is vertical at the NAIRU – there is no permanent trade-off.
AO4
Counterarguments / evaluation: (i) Supply shocks can shift the SRPC, producing stagflation –
simultaneous rising inflation and rising unemployment, or falling inflation with flat unemployment,
which a naive Phillips curve cannot explain. The 2022–24 UK data arguably reflects this: most of the
inflation was externally driven (energy, post-Covid supply chains, £/$ depreciation) rather than
demand-pull. (ii) Anchored inflation expectations (from 25 years of BoE inflation targeting) may
mean that expectations did not de-anchor, avoiding a wage-price spiral and allowing inflation to fall
without a significant rise in unemployment. (iii) Structural changes in labour markets – gig economy,
weaker unions – may have flattened the SRPC, so even tight markets do not produce strong wage
inflation. (iv) Inflation is multi-causal: exchange rate, commodity prices, productivity, expectations all
matter. (v) Still, the Phillips framework – particularly the expectations-augmented version – remains
useful for understanding why monetary policy must respond to prevent second-round effects.
Overall: the framework retains analytical value, especially in augmented form, but the 2022–24 data
highlight its limitations in the face of large supply shocks.


                                © Predicted Paper – in the style of WJEC/Eduqas

Allow other relevant points.

25. Discuss whether the HDI alone is sufficient to conclude that Saudi Arabia has a higher
level of development than Costa Rica. [8]


 Ba                 AO2                              AO3                               AO4
 nd

  3                                    3 marks                            3 marks
                                       Excellent analysis                 Excellent evaluation
                                       Well-developed chain               Supported judgement that the
                                       explaining how different           HDI alone is insufficient, using
                                       development indicators point       multiple dimensions of
                                       to different conclusions.          development.

  2    2 marks                         2 marks                            2 marks
       Good application                Good analysis                      Good evaluation
       Data used effectively on both   Developed chain of reasoning       Developed counterarguments
       sides.                          about HDI strengths.               about HDI's limitations.

  1    1 mark                          1 mark                             1 mark
       Limited application.            Limited analysis.                  Limited evaluation.

  0    0 marks                         0 marks                            0 marks


Indicative content
AO2
HDI suggests Saudi Arabia is more developed:
HDI 0.875 vs 0.806 – substantial margin (Very High vs Very High, but Saudi Arabia is well inside the
Very High band). GNI/capita (PPP$) is more than double ($54,992 vs $23,909). Longer life
expectancy (78.8 vs 77.3) and more mean years of schooling (11.3 vs 8.8).
Broader indicators suggest Costa Rica may be more developed:
Gender Inequality Index: Costa Rica 0.256 (much lower, i.e. more gender equal) vs Saudi Arabia
0.569 – Saudi Arabia has very large gender gaps in labour participation, health, empowerment. Gini
coefficient: slightly lower in Saudi Arabia (0.459) but both moderately unequal; however Saudi
Arabia's distributional figures mask the precarious position of migrant workers. Costa Rica has
stronger democratic institutions, no standing army, and historically high investment in health and
education.
AO3
Development involves more than income, health, and education. Amartya Sen's capabilities
approach emphasises the real freedoms people have to achieve valuable outcomes. HDI captures
only three dimensions, and each is imperfectly measured: GNI includes oil rents (a large share of
Saudi GDP) which may not translate into broad-based welfare; life expectancy does not capture
quality of life or morbidity; schooling years do not measure learning outcomes.
AO4
Counterarguments: (i) HDI is a composite index chosen for its data availability and simplicity – it is a
starting point, not a full picture. (ii) Inequality-adjusted HDI (IHDI) applies discounts for inequality in
each dimension and typically cuts Saudi Arabia's score more than Costa Rica's. (iii) Environmental
sustainability – Costa Rica is a global leader (>99% renewable electricity, high biodiversity
protection) whereas Saudi Arabia is a major hydrocarbon economy. (iv) Freedoms – political, civil,
gender – are highly unequal. Costa Rica scores strongly on press freedom and democracy indices.
(v) Conversely, Saudi Arabia has significantly better physical infrastructure and healthcare systems
in some dimensions. (vi) Other metrics: MPI (Multidimensional Poverty Index), Happy Planet Index,

                               © Predicted Paper – in the style of WJEC/Eduqas
                                                             MARKING SCHEME – A520U10-1 (Predicted – Hard)

Social Progress Index would give fuller pictures. Overall judgement: HDI alone is not sufficient;
when the broader picture (inequality, gender, environment, freedoms) is taken into account, the
claim that Saudi Arabia is 'more developed' becomes much more contestable.
Allow other relevant points.


                               © Predicted Paper – in the style of WJEC/Eduqas

26. Using supply and demand diagram(s), discuss whether the data suggests that oil prices
are more likely to rise or fall in the future. [9]


 Ba                AO1                                  AO2                            AO3 / AO4
 nd

  3                                                                          3 marks (AO4)
                                                                             Well-reasoned supported
                                                                             judgement about the direction
                                                                             of oil prices, accounting for
                                                                             short vs long run.

  2    2 marks                            2 marks                            2 marks (AO3)
       Accurate S&D diagrams              Effective use of data on both      Developed analysis of one
       illustrating both sides.           sides.                             side. 2 marks (AO4)
                                                                             Developed counterargument.

  1    1 mark                             1 mark                             1 mark (AO3) / 1 mark (AO4)
       Diagrams with inaccuracies or      Data used on one side only.        Chain of reasoning
       one side only.                                                        underdeveloped.

  0    0 marks                            0 marks                            0 marks


Indicative content
AO1
Two diagrams: Rising prices – S shifts left (OPEC+ cuts) and/or D shifts right (EM demand), giving
higher P and lower Q. Falling prices – S shifts right (US shale, EV transition reducing future
demand) and/or D shifts left (recession, EV adoption), giving lower P and higher Q.
AO2
UPWARD price pressures:
OPEC+ voluntary supply cuts of 1 mb/d shift supply curve left. Geopolitical tensions in the Middle
East add risk premium and threaten disruption of a major producing region. Strong demand growth
from India and Southeast Asia as incomes rise (YED > 0 for oil in industrialising economies).
DOWNWARD price pressures:
US shale production at record 13.2 mb/d expanding supply, partially offsetting OPEC+ cuts.
Accelerating EV adoption (17% of new car sales) and IEA forecast of demand peaking by 2030 – a
structural negative demand shock. Potential recession in advanced economies reduces industrial
demand.
AO3
Developed chain (downward pressure): EV substitution reduces derived demand for oil as transport
fuel → demand curve for oil shifts left → at current price there is excess supply → producers cut
prices to clear the market. Amplified by secular policies (carbon pricing, EV subsidies, infrastructure
build-out). Additionally, US shale is very responsive (higher PES than conventional oil) so supply
shifts further right as technology improves.
AO4
Counter (upward pressure): OPEC+ has historically defended price floors through coordinated cuts,
and Saudi Arabia's 1 mb/d voluntary reduction plus the Middle East risk premium can be
substantial. Oil demand remains inelastic in the short run – even with EVs expanding, total oil
demand is still growing, and emerging market growth outweighs OECD decline for several more
years. Timing considerations: recession and EV adoption may dominate in the medium term, but
short-run OPEC+ action can produce spikes. The chart shows that oil has traded in a wide range
                                  © Predicted Paper – in the style of WJEC/Eduqas
                                                             MARKING SCHEME – A520U10-1 (Predicted – Hard)

($75–$123) over the past two years – volatility is the norm, making a one-directional forecast
unreliable. PES is low in the short run (wells take years to drill) which amplifies price swings from
demand shocks. Overall: short-run prices likely volatile with upward skew from OPEC+/geopolitics,
but long-run structural decline as electrification gathers pace. Judgement must recognise this two-
horizon picture.
Allow other relevant points.
